beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The Mycroft approach teaches computational finance by making students solve each problem three ways: Excel, Python, and LLM prompts. When the three methods diverge, like an IRR of 12.5, 12.4, and 12.8 percent, students must diagnose which is correct and why. The capital allocation line connects the risk-free asset to the optimal risky portfolio, letting investors pick a point matching risk tolerance. Its derivation is rigorous, but mean-variance optimization is unstable because estimation error corrupts the inputs.

artifactLines: The Mycroft approach teaches computational finance by making students solve each problem three ways: Excel, Python, and LLM prompts.
When the three methods diverge, like an IRR of 12.5, 12.4, and 12.8 percent, students must diagnose which is correct and why.
The capital allocation line connects the risk-free asset to the optimal risky portfolio, letting investors pick a point matching risk tolerance.
Its derivation is rigorous, but mean-variance optimization is unstable because estimation error corrupts the inputs.

beats.narration_text: The triangulated problem sets, solving one IRR in Excel, Python, and an LLM then diagnosing why 12.5, 12.4, and 12.8 percent diverge, is exactly the discipline I want to practice. I also want to connect it to the capital allocation line derivation you cited. Can you (1) work the CAL algebra from R equals one-minus-y times R_f plus y times R_p through to the linear risk-return line, (2) build one triangulated IRR problem with a real cash-flow stream, and (3) explain which of the three tools is most trustworthy and why?

beats.narration_text: Core Claim: Capital allocation line (CAL) connects risk-free asset to optimal risky portfolio; investors select point on CAL matching risk tolerance; leverage (borrowing at risk-free rate) enables higher returns than tangency portfolio alone; mean-variance optimization unstable due to estimation error. Methodological Soundness: CAL derivation rigorous: portfolio return R = (1-y)R_f + yR_p, volatility σ = y·σ_p (risk-free asset has σ=0), eliminating y yields linear R vs.

beats.narration_text: Design triangulated problem sets where students solve the same problem in Excel, Python, and via LLM prompts, then diagnose any discrepancies (e.g., “Calculate IRR for this cash flow stream using all three methods. When Excel returns 12.5%, Python returns 12.4%, and the LLM returns 12.8%, explain which is correct and why the others differ.”)
